name: Randy Sandke
abstract: Jay Randall Sandke (born May 5, 1949 in Chicago, Illinois) is a jazz trumpeter and guitarist. While a student at Indiana University in 1968, he and Michael Brecker started a jazz-rock band (Mrs.

quotes.text: Yeah. I mean I don’t know that that’s so much a generational thing because I think it’s always been like that. There’ve been a few players that can just make it sing and take wings and whatever, and a lot of people for whatever reason, it’s just less than that. And there are some players these days that I think are quite lyrical, and even younger players, and then guys who are not. One thing, going back to another thing about playing, one thing that sort of interests me, I mean I guess is that Louis Armstrong for instance, he always, he could play with the worst band in the world and at times — not the worst band but guys that were just on a totally different — I mean nobody was on his level, but I mean like way under. And he would say that he didn’t even hear the band. Like he would just play, he just had such a strong feeling inside him that he would just charge ahead and it would just fall into place. And he could do that. And of course, that’s him. I cannot do that. I mean I can do that to a certain extent, but I always feel very much like I’m playing WITH the guys and that my playing is affected, for better or worse, by what’s going on around me. And sometimes I feel like it’s very difficult for me to like really get it happening if it doesn’t seem like it is around me. You know I’ll try, but I mean I guess that’s the nature of the thing. Sometimes you feel like it’s work, other times it’s just happening by itself. And it doesn’t necessarily, there are so many variables. It’s not just even the caliber of the musicians. The night after last we were doing this thing at Carnegie Hall and I played a few small group things. The rhythm section was terrific. Some of the tunes felt really good. The last tune we did, it just felt like — I mean I don’t know if the audience perceived this. But it just felt like for whatever reason it just was not coming together, it was an effort all the way through. And it sounded okay, I mean nobody did any obvious goofs. But to me it just didn’t really have the feeling. It wasn’t comfortable. And of course like when you don’t feel comfortable, there’s always a tendency to blame yourself. I think when you’ve got experience you tend to notice a little bit more that it’s other things too. But you just get into so many variables — the weather, how people are feeling, the acoustics.
quotes.caption: Randy Sandke recalls a difficult small group performance at Carnegie Hall
quotes.doc_title: Randy Sandke interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 10, 1997

quotes.text: Well I mean there’s a lot of reasons for it. For one thing I think the critics have at least gone to music school and know something about music. For another thing, well it’s already institutionalized. There is funding, there is money, the whole structure of it. I mean just briefly one example, I mean I’ve had this kind of running battle with BMI, who I’m a member of, because they don’t pay for live performances of jazz, but they pay for live performances of classical music. So in other words, for instance, like two nights ago I wrote this huge thing for the Carnegie Hall Jazz Band. I mean it was an arrangement so I wouldn’t have technically gotten paid. But I have had jazz compositions performed at Lincoln Center and Carnegie Hall. I don’t make a dime. I can have a score that’s like an inch thick. But I go out to Tulsa, Oklahoma with a regional symphony orchestra and play a classical piece, and I get a nice check in the mail. So I mean that’s just an example of the way society, here in the form of BMI, is encouraging — because to get paid for your work, that’s encouragement — is encouraging classical music. Whereas if it’s jazz, if it’s even called jazz, even if it’s almost the same piece, you’re treated totally differently. Also I just find a lot of — I think in classical music there’s more openness to do new things, and especially, the irony of the whole thing is that basically I’m still, even when I’m doing things that are what I consider on my idea, the cutting edge, I’m still basically conservative. So that for classical musical, like the stuff that I write, even though it’s harmonically maybe to the point of atonal, it’s still very melodic, it still has form you can follow. It’s not crazy, formless. I try very hard to make it —
quotes.caption: Randy Sandke discusses BMI payment disparities for jazz vs classical
quotes.doc_title: Randy Sandke interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 10, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Scott Robinson
about_quotes.evidence: Randy Sandke does a lot of Bix’s music and Trumbauer and all of that, and he knows I play the C melody too, so I get to sort of be the Trumbauer-Rollini amalgam in those concerts.

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: They see Randy Sandke. They see Randy Brecker. They see and hear Peter Erskine. They see John Clayton. They see John’s partner, Jeff Hamilton. These things have been the kind of thing that I’ve tried to set up in a way to bring to fruition the dream of a program that is strong.
